content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A team trimmed 38% of the tokens flowing through their coding agent. Their bill went up 6.8%.
That’s not a typo and it’s not an edge case — it’s a finding from a 2026 study of 2,848 provider-billed Claude Code runs. The “obvious” cost lever, cutting tokens, made things worse.
The reason is almost embarrassing once you see it: the compression rewrote content sitting inside a cached prompt prefix. Every rewrite invalidated the cache. Cache reads were the cheap, 90%-off tokens — the “optimized” version paid full price for them instead.
The two levers with numbers you can bank on
Here’s the piece that actually moves the needle, and it’s boring by comparison: prompt caching and batch processing. Anthropic and OpenAI both publish fixed discount percentages for these two, and they stack. A cache read costs 90% less than a fresh input token. Batch processing — accepting a result within 24 hours instead of instantly — cuts every token 50%. Combine them and the discount compounds to 95%. On Claude Sonnet 5’s current intro pricing, that’s $0.10 per million tokens instead of $2.
I don’t love citing vendor-published discount tables as if they’re guaranteed savings, so here’s a number from outside the vendors. A…
